target.title: fix(state): refuse chmod-less agent database volumes that cannot prove credential privacy
target.body:
## Summary

The per-agent SQLite database stores credentials (API keys and OAuth tokens under `agents/<agentId>/agent/`). On filesystems that reject `chmod` (Azure Files, NFS without `no_root_squash`, some Docker/SMB volume drivers), `ensureOpenClawAgentDatabasePermissions` called raw `chmodSync` and the agent could not open its database at all: it threw an uncaught `ENOTSUP`/`EPERM` before the handle opened, while the non-secret shared state DB came up fine.

This is the follow-up to #92786, which closed that gap by reusing the shared state DB helper `bestEffortChmodSync`. @vincentkoc closed #92786 because that helper is too permissive for a secret store: it would silently open a world-readable credential store on a chmod-less volume. The canonical paths named were an ACL-aware Windows verify, or a scoped policy that refuses unsafe agent DB locations. This PR implements the scoped fail-closed policy; the shared non-secret state DB is untouched and keeps its best-effort behavior.

For Windows specifically, where file modes cannot represent NTFS ACLs, this implements the scoped option (B) from the review thread: trust only the default per-user profile agent DB location, which is private by the Windows per-user profile ACL, and refuse a relocated `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR` store, which is where the network-backed/SMB exposure lives. A native ACL-aware verify (option A, for example `icacls`) is a tracked follow-up and would only widen what Windows accepts; POSIX behavior is unchanged either way.

## Fix

New `enforcePrivateAgentDbChmodSync` used for the agent DB directory and sidecar files, replacing the raw `chmodSync` calls. It proves the resulting mode is private after every `chmod` attempt, on both the success and the failure path, before the credential store is trusted:

- POSIX: after attempting `chmod`, OpenClaw reads the resulting mode with `stat` regardless of whether `chmod` reported success or threw. The store is tolerated only when `stat` proves it private (`mode & 0o077 === 0`) for the directory and each existing DB/WAL/SHM target; otherwise it refuses with a scoped, actionable error pointing the operator at a local path via `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`. When `chmod` failed but the target is already private the chmod was cosmetic, so OpenClaw warns once and continues.
- Windows (`process.platform === "win32"`): the decision is by location, not by `chmod`. Windows file modes do not reflect NTFS ACLs, so a `chmod` result (success or failure) proves nothing about credential privacy; the prior revision trusted any successful Windows `chmod`, which left a relocated SMB store accepted while it was still group/world accessible. OpenClaw now trusts the default per-user profile agent DB location (private by the Windows per-user profile ACL) and refuses a relocated store, an explicit `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR` placed outside the default per-user agents root, where the network-backed/SMB exposure lives. A shared helper `isDefaultProfileAgentDatabaseLocation` classifies the location, and both the read-write open path and the read-only loader use it, so a relocated Windows credential store is refused before it is read. This also stops the prior revision from over-refusing the default location when a Windows `chmod` merely failed.

This closes the gap raised in review: a volume can report a successful `chmod` while leaving the mode unchanged. Core CIFS mounted without the Unix extensions documents exactly this; `man mount.cifs` states that attempting to change permissions "will return success but have no effect", and `stat` then reports the mount `file_mode`/`dir_mode` (commonly `0644`/`0755`). Silent-ignore FUSE/bindfs mounts behave the same way. The previous helper only checked the resulting mode inside the `catch` path, so a silent-success volume sailed past the check and opened a `0644` credential DB. Verifying after every attempt refuses that case.

This is deliberately stricter than the sibling `bestEffortChmodSync`, which tolerates `ENOTSUP`/`EOPNOTSUPP`/`EINVAL` unconditionally for the non-secret store. For a credential store the only safe question is whether the secret is private now.

Every secret-bearing open path shares one gate. The read-only auth-profile loader (`readAuthProfileJsonCellReadOnly` in `src/agents/auth-profiles/sqlite.ts`, reached from `loadPersistedAuthProfileStore` -> `readPersistedAuthProfileStoreRaw`) previously opened the same `openclaw-agent.sqlite` with `DatabaseSync(..., { readOnly: true })` with no privacy check, so an existing `0644` credential DB from a chmod-less/silent-success volume was still read and trusted at auth-resolution time. Both the read-write open path and the read-only loader now run the existing DB/WAL/SHM files through one exported helper `enforcePrivateAgentDbFilePermissions`, so a store that cannot be proven private is refused before it is read, not only before it is written.

The post-open permission pass runs inside the same cleanup as schema setup. The refusal can fire on the second (post-open) pass when the agent directory is private but the freshly created DB file is born group/world readable on a chmod-less mount; keeping it inside the cleanup closes the live SQLite handle and WAL maintenance before the error escapes instead of leaking them. This PR is rebased on `main`; the open path now uses the `configureSqliteConnectionPragmas` cleanup added in ea346f4361, with the post-open permission pass moved inside that same `try` so a privacy refusal still releases the handle.

## Verification

- Colocated regression suite `src/state/openclaw-agent-db.permissions.test.ts` (14 cases): chmod success hardens to `0700`/`0600`; chmod fails but store already private opens; chmod reports success but the store stays world-readable refuses (the silent-ignore case); chmod fails on a non-private dir refuses; `EACCES` on a non-private dir refuses; a post-open refusal closes the freshly opened handle (asserts every captured `DatabaseSync` instance reports `!isOpen`); a private store still loads through the read-only auth-profile path; and a silent-success world-readable store refuses on that same read-only path. For the Windows policy: a relocated agent DB on `win32` refuses with the resulting handle released; the default per-user location is trusted on `win32` even when `chmod` cannot harden it; and three `isDefaultProfileAgentDatabaseLocation` cases prove the default per-user path is trusted, a relocated `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR` store is untrusted, and a path outside the default agents root is untrusted. The post-open case forces the target non-private through the resolved DB path so it is deterministic regardless of the ambient umask. Run with `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/state/openclaw-agent-db.permissions.test.ts` -> 14 passed.
- `oxlint` on the changed files -> clean.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Opening the per-agent credential database on a volume that does not apply `chmod` now opens only when the store is provably private, and otherwise refuses with a scoped error instead of silently storing credentials with permissive modes. This includes volumes that report a successful `chmod` while leaving the mode unchanged, which previously opened a world-readable credential DB. A refusal that fires after the database handle is already open closes that handle and its WAL maintenance instead of leaking them. The read-only auth-profile load path (`loadPersistedAuthProfileStore` -> `readPersistedAuthProfileStoreRaw`) shares the same gate, so an existing `0644` credential store is refused before it is read, not just before it is written.

Real environment tested: Linux (Node 22.19) running the production entry point `openOpenClawAgentDatabase` against a real temporary state dir. The chmod-less volume is reproduced at the OS layer with an LD_PRELOAD shim over libc chmod(2)/fchmod(2)/fchmodat(2): with `CHMOD_FAIL_CODE=SILENT` the calls return success without changing the mode (the documented CIFS-without-unix-extensions and silent-ignore FUSE behavior); with `ENOTSUP`/`EACCES` they return the matching real errno from the kernel call boundary. Store privacy is varied with the process umask (077 produces a private 0700/0600 store; 022 produces a group/world readable 0644 DB file that only becomes visible to the post-open permission pass). After each run the resulting agent DB file mode is read with `stat`, and open file descriptors are counted from `/proc/self/fd` to show whether the SQLite handle leaked.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: `gcc -shared -fPIC -o chmodfail.so chmodfail.c`; then for each open-path scenario `( umask <077|022>; LD_PRELOAD=$PWD/chmodfail.so CHMOD_FAIL_CODE=<SILENT|ENOTSUP|EACCES> node_modules/.bin/tsx drive-agent-db.mts )` where the driver calls the real `openOpenClawAgentDatabase`, prints the resulting DB file mode on success, and on a thrown refusal scans `/proc/self/fd` for any descriptor still pointing at `openclaw-agent.sqlite`. For the read-only path, seed a credential store with `DRIVE_MODE=seed node_modules/.bin/tsx drive-read-auth.mts` (then leave its DB file at `0644`) and load it with `SEED_DIR=<dir> LD_PRELOAD=$PWD/chmodfail.so CHMOD_FAIL_CODE=SILENT node_modules/.bin/tsx drive-read-auth.mts`, which calls the real `readPersistedAuthProfileStoreRaw`. For the Windows policy, seed a relocated store the same way, then load it through `drive-read-auth-win32.mts`, which overrides `process.platform` to `win32` before calling the same real `readPersistedAuthProfileStoreRaw`: `SEED_DIR=<dir> WIN32=1 LD_PRELOAD=$PWD/chmodfail.so CHMOD_FAIL_CODE=SILENT node_modules/.bin/tsx drive-read-auth-win32.mts`. Each before capture reverts the changed source files to the prior baseline (the open-path/read-path before captures revert to the prior PR head; the Windows before capture reverts both `src/state/openclaw-agent-db.ts` and `src/agents/auth-profiles/sqlite.ts` to `origin/main`, which has no privacy gate); the same shim is used for before and after.

Evidence after fix: redacted live terminal output (non-secret `state/db` best-effort warn lines trimmed).

```
# BEFORE (reviewed head da170353), SILENT volume (chmod returns success, no effect), umask 022
#   -> opens a world/group-readable 0644 credential DB (the reported gap):
$ ( umask 022; LD_PRELOAD=$PWD/chmodfail.so CHMOD_FAIL_CODE=SILENT node_modules/.bin/tsx drive-agent-db.mts )
agent DB open: OPENED db.isOpen=true path=<STATE_DIR>/agents/main/agent/openclaw-agent.sqlite
agent DB file mode after open: 0644
agent DB dir mode after open: 0700
OPEN_DB_FDS=3
REAL_EXIT=0

# AFTER (this PR), SILENT volume, umask 022 -> refuses, no leaked descriptors:
$ ( umask 022; LD_PRELOAD=$PWD/chmodfail.so CHMOD_FAIL_CODE=SILENT node_modules/.bin/tsx drive-agent-db.mts )
agent DB open: REFUSED - OpenClaw agent database <STATE_DIR>/agents/main/agent/openclaw-agent.sqlite stores credentials and cannot be made private on this volume (mode 644; chmod reported success without changing the mode). Move the agent database to a filesystem that enforces private permissions by setting OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR to a local directory.
OPEN_DB_FDS=0
REAL_EXIT=1

# AFTER (this PR), ENOTSUP volume, private store (umask 077) -> opens:
$ ( umask 077; LD_PRELOAD=$PWD/chmodfail.so CHMOD_FAIL_CODE=ENOTSUP node_modules/.bin/tsx drive-agent-db.mts )
agent DB open: OPENED db.isOpen=true path=<STATE_DIR>/agents/main/agent/openclaw-agent.sqlite
agent DB file mode after open: 0600
agent DB dir mode after open: 0700
OPEN_DB_FDS=3
REAL_EXIT=0

# AFTER (this PR), ENOTSUP volume, group/world readable DB file (umask 022) -> post-open refuses, handle closed:
$ ( umask 022; LD_PRELOAD=$PWD/chmodfail.so CHMOD_FAIL_CODE=ENOTSUP node_modules/.bin/tsx drive-agent-db.mts )
agent DB open: REFUSED - OpenClaw agent database <STATE_DIR>/agents/main/agent/openclaw-agent.sqlite stores credentials and cannot be made private on this volume (mode 644; Error: ENOTSUP: operation not supported on socket, chmod '<STATE_DIR>/agents/main/agent/openclaw-agent.sqlite'). Move the agent database to a filesystem that enforces private permissions by setting OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR to a local directory.
OPEN_DB_FDS=0
REAL_EXIT=1

# AFTER (this PR), EACCES volume, group/world readable DB file (umask 022) -> refuses (stays loud), handle closed:
$ ( umask 022; LD_PRELOAD=$PWD/chmodfail.so CHMOD_FAIL_CODE=EACCES node_modules/.bin/tsx drive-agent-db.mts )
agent DB open: REFUSED - OpenClaw agent database <STATE_DIR>/agents/main/agent/openclaw-agent.sqlite stores credentials and cannot be made private on this volume (mode 644; Error: EACCES: permission denied, chmod '<STATE_DIR>/agents/main/agent/openclaw-agent.sqlite'). Move the agent database to a filesystem that enforces private permissions by setting OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR to a local directory.
OPEN_DB_FDS=0
REAL_EXIT=1

# AFTER (this PR), normal local filesystem (no shim), umask 022 -> opens at 0600 (no regression):
$ ( umask 022; node_modules/.bin/tsx drive-agent-db.mts )
agent DB open: OPENED db.isOpen=true path=<STATE_DIR>/agents/main/agent/openclaw-agent.sqlite
agent DB file mode after open: 0600
agent DB dir mode after open: 0700
OPEN_DB_FDS=3
REAL_EXIT=0

# Read-only auth-profile load path (loadPersistedAuthProfileStore -> readPersistedAuthProfileStoreRaw),
# SILENT volume (chmod returns success, no effect), against the same existing 0644 credential DB.
# BEFORE (prior PR head, open-path fix only) -> the read-only loader trusts the 0644 store:
$ ( SEED_DIR=<STATE_DIR> LD_PRELOAD=$PWD/chmodfail.so CHMOD_FAIL_CODE=SILENT node_modules/.bin/tsx drive-read-auth.mts )
credential store file mode before read: 0644
read-only auth load: TRUSTED store (profiles=anthropic)
REAL_EXIT=0

# AFTER (this PR) -> the same read-only loader refuses the 0644 store before opening it:
$ ( SEED_DIR=<STATE_DIR> LD_PRELOAD=$PWD/chmodfail.so CHMOD_FAIL_CODE=SILENT node_modules/.bin/tsx drive-read-auth.mts )
credential store file mode before read: 0644
read-only auth load: REFUSED - OpenClaw agent database <STATE_DIR>/agents/main/agent/openclaw-agent.sqlite stores credentials and cannot be made private on this volume (mode 644; chmod reported success without changing the mode). Move the agent database to a filesystem that enforces private permissions by setting OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR to a local directory.
REAL_EXIT=1

# Windows policy (process.platform forced to win32) against a relocated credential store
# on a SILENT volume (chmod returns success, no effect), reading the same existing 0644 DB.
# BEFORE (origin/main, no privacy gate) -> the relocated Windows store is read and trusted:
$ ( SEED_DIR=<STATE_DIR> WIN32=1 LD_PRELOAD=$PWD/chmodfail.so CHMOD_FAIL_CODE=SILENT node_modules/.bin/tsx drive-read-auth-win32.mts )
platform=win32; relocated credential store file mode: 0644
read-only auth load: TRUSTED store (profiles=anthropic)
REAL_EXIT=0

# AFTER (this PR) -> the relocated Windows store is refused before it is opened:
$ ( SEED_DIR=<STATE_DIR> WIN32=1 LD_PRELOAD=$PWD/chmodfail.so CHMOD_FAIL_CODE=SILENT node_modules/.bin/tsx drive-read-auth-win32.mts )
platform=win32; relocated credential store file mode: 0644
read-only auth load: REFUSED - OpenClaw agent database <STATE_DIR>/agents/main/agent/openclaw-agent.sqlite stores credentials in a relocated Windows location whose private NTFS ACL cannot be proven (Windows file modes do not reflect ACLs). Keep agent credential storage at the default per-user location instead of relocating it with OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR.
REAL_EXIT=1
```

Observed result after fix: On a volume that does not apply `chmod`, including one that reports `chmod` success while leaving the mode unchanged, the agent DB opens (exit 0) only when the credential store is provably private; when it would be group or world readable it refuses with a scoped, actionable error (exit 1) instead of storing credentials permissively. On the reviewed head da170353 the same SILENT volume opened a 0644 credential DB (exit 0). When the refusal fires after the handle is open, the descriptor count to `openclaw-agent.sqlite` is 0, so the SQLite handle and WAL maintenance are released. A genuine EACCES fault stays loud. On a normal local filesystem the store opens at 0600. The read-only auth-profile loader behaves identically: against the same `0644` store the prior PR head trusted it (exit 0), and this revision refuses it (exit 1) before opening, so a non-private credential store cannot be read either. On Windows the same relocated credential store is refused before it is read (exit 1) where `origin/main` reads and trusts it (exit 0), while the default per-user profile location stays trusted.

What was not tested: A literal Azure Files / NFS / Docker volume, a native Windows SMB mount, or a real CIFS `dynperm` mount end to end. The chmod-less behavior is reproduced at the libc syscall boundary via LD_PRELOAD, matching the documented CIFS-without-unix-extensions contract; `dynperm` (where the client caches a fabricated mode that `stat` reports but the server does not persist) is a CIFS-specific case a mode check cannot defeat and is out of scope. The Windows policy is exercised by overriding `process.platform` to `win32`, both in the regression suite and in the live `drive-read-auth-win32.mts` driver, on Linux: a relocated store is refused before it is read and the default per-user location is trusted. Real NTFS ACL semantics and a native Windows SMB mount were not exercised on Linux; the policy trusts the per-user profile ACL by contract rather than reading the ACL, which a native ACL-aware verify (option A) would add as a follow-up.

Refs: #92786 (closed; this is its canonical follow-up), #91919 (the shared state DB best-effort fix this intentionally does not reuse for the secret store), ea346f4361 (the failed-init cleanup this PR is rebased onto). CIFS contract: https://man7.org/linux/man-pages/man8/mount.cifs.8.html

CC @vincentkoc for the canonical-fix review.

target.title: feat(kimi): show code quota in usage status
target.body:
## Summary

Adds Kimi Code usage reporting to the existing provider usage pipeline.

- Adds a Kimi usage fetcher for `GET https://api.kimi.com/coding/v1/usages`.
- Parses Kimi Code `5h` and `7d` quota windows into the existing usage snapshot shape.
- Wires the bundled `kimi-coding` provider into `resolveUsageAuth` and `fetchUsageSnapshot`.
- Maps `kimi-code` and `kimi-coding` usage aliases to the new `kimi` usage provider.
- Surfaces Kimi API-key usage windows in `models.authStatus`, matching the existing DeepSeek API-key status enrichment path.

## Why

Kimi Code subscription users currently do not see quota usage in OpenClaw status surfaces. This is a focused follow-up to #67593.

A previous broader attempt in #20343 also tried to add Kimi/Moonshot usage, but it mixed unrelated quota UI and provider changes and was closed unmerged. This PR keeps scope limited to Kimi Code usage integration so `/status`, provider usage summaries, and plugin usage hooks can show the Kimi Code quota windows without changing unrelated providers or UI grouping behavior.

## Behavior

When a Kimi API key is available from the provider config/store or `KIMI_CODE_API_KEY`, `KIMI_API_KEY`, or `KIMICODE_API_KEY`, OpenClaw can fetch the Kimi Code usage payload and display:

- `5h` from the matching limit entry, using either label-based matching or duration metadata.
- `7d` from the top-level `usage` row.

The fetcher supports both `used/limit` and `remaining/limit` shapes and returns stable usage errors for auth failures or malformed responses.

The Kimi Code usage endpoint response shape is treated as a provider endpoint contract assumption: the live proof below verifies the current `/coding/v1/usages` behavior, while maintainers still own the decision to accept that external contract for bundled status reporting.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** Kimi Code subscription quota usage should be fetched through the new Kimi provider usage integration and normalized into the existing OpenClaw provider usage snapshot, so status/usage surfaces can show the Kimi `5h` and `7d` windows instead of omitting Kimi.

**Real environment tested:** Local macOS OpenClaw checkout on PR branch `codex/kimi-code-usage-quota` at commit `1858f38c`, using the real local OpenClaw config at `/Users/alex/.openclaw/openclaw.json` with provider `kimi-coding` base URL `https://api.kimi.com/coding` and a real Kimi Code API key from config. Token redacted.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran a local Node command with `node --import tsx` that imported this PR's `./src/infra/provider-usage.fetch.kimi.ts`, read the local OpenClaw Kimi provider config, and called `fetchKimiUsage(token, 10000, fetch, { baseUrl })` against the real Kimi Code usage endpoint.

**Evidence after fix:** Terminal output from the live command after this patch:

```json
{
  "proof": "real Kimi Code usage endpoint via PR fetchKimiUsage",
  "startedAt": "2026-06-13T09:25:23.283Z",
  "finishedAt": "2026-06-13T09:25:23.435Z",
  "baseUrl": "https://api.kimi.com/coding",
  "provider": "kimi",
  "displayName": "Kimi",
  "windows": [
    {
      "label": "5h",
      "usedPercent": 8
    },
    {
      "label": "7d",
      "usedPercent": 3
    }
  ],
  "error": null,
  "token": "<redacted>"
}
```

**Observed result after fix:** The real Kimi Code endpoint returned a successful `kimi` usage snapshot with `displayName: "Kimi"`, parsed quota windows `5h: 8%` and `7d: 3%`, and `error: null`. This verifies the PR's live fetcher can authenticate with a real Kimi Code setup and normalize live endpoint output into the provider usage shape consumed by OpenClaw status/usage surfaces.

**What was not tested:** Full UI rendering was not exercised; this proof covers the live provider usage fetch path and parsed snapshot used by the status/usage pipeline.
## Validation

- `git diff --check`
- `COREPACK_HOME=/tmp/corepack corepack pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 docs/.generated/plugin-sdk-api-baseline.sha256 extensions/kimi-coding/index.ts extensions/kimi-coding/index.test.ts src/infra/provider-usage.fetch.ts src/infra/provider-usage.fetch.kimi.ts src/infra/provider-usage.fetch.kimi.test.ts src/infra/provider-usage.load.test.ts src/infra/provider-usage.shared.ts src/infra/provider-usage.shared.test.ts src/infra/provider-usage.types.ts src/plugin-sdk/provider-usage.ts`

target.title: fix(gateway): stop accepting the Control UI token via ?token= query string
target.body:
## Summary

The Control UI assistant-media route (`/__openclaw__/assistant-media`) accepted the gateway shared secret — and paired operator device tokens — from a `?token=` URL query parameter (`allowQueryToken: true` in `src/gateway/control-ui.ts`). Secrets in URLs leak through reverse-proxy/CDN access logs, browser history, and the `Referer` header, and the gateway token is long-lived.

This contradicts the rest of the codebase:
- the hooks endpoint hard-rejects query tokens (`src/gateway/server/hooks-request-handler.ts`), and
- the Control UI itself warns that `?token=` "may appear in server logs" and prefers the URL fragment (`ui/src/ui/app-settings.ts`).

The legitimate browser flow doesn't need it: an authenticated `meta` request mints a signed, source-scoped, short-TTL `mediaTicket`, and the media fetch uses `?mediaTicket=`; programmatic clients send `Authorization: Bearer`. The first-party UI already uses `mediaTicket` exclusively.

### Change
- Delete `resolveAssistantMediaAuthToken` and `resolveControlUiReadAuthToken` and the `allowQueryToken` plumbing; Control UI read auth now resolves the token from `getBearerToken` only (header), or the signed `mediaTicket`. Net `control-ui.ts`: fewer paths.
- Existing media tests moved from `?token=` to `Authorization: Bearer`; the redundant query-auth device-token test removed (its header equivalent already exists).
- New e2e regression asserts a **correct** token in `?token=` is rejected while header + `mediaTicket` still serve media.

## Verification

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/control-ui.http.test.ts` → 220 passed
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/control-ui-assistant-media-query-token.e2e.test.ts` → 1 passed
- `oxfmt --check` + `oxlint` on changed files → clean; `pnpm tsgo:core` → 0 errors

### Real behavior proof
- Behavior addressed: the Control UI assistant-media route accepted the gateway shared secret (and paired operator device tokens) via the `?token=` URL query string, leaking a long-lived secret into reverse-proxy/CDN access logs, browser history, and the `Referer` header.
- Real environment tested: a real OpenClaw gateway HTTP server booted on loopback TCP via the gateway e2e harness (`withGatewayServer` → `startGatewayServer`); requests issued with real `fetch` against `http://127.0.0.1:<port>`. Node 22.22, Linux (WSL2).
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: drove one probe against a live gateway twice — first with the pre-fix file (`git show origin/main:src/gateway/control-ui.ts`), then with this PR's file — issuing `GET /__openclaw__/assistant-media` five ways via `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs`.
- Evidence after fix: live console output captured from the running gateway (`node` server, real loopback HTTP), before (pre-fix `origin/main`) vs after (this PR):
```text
BEFORE  origin/main src/gateway/control-ui.ts   (real gateway @ 127.0.0.1:62000)
  [1] no credential                    -> HTTP 401
  [2] ?token=<CORRECT gateway secret>  -> HTTP 200   secret-in-URL ACCEPTED  (the leak)
  [3] Authorization: Bearer <token>    -> HTTP 200
  [4] meta=1 (Bearer) -> mediaTicket   -> HTTP 200   available=true  ticket=v1.eyJzY29wZSI...
  [5] ?mediaTicket=<signed ticket>     -> HTTP 200

AFTER   this PR  src/gateway/control-ui.ts       (real gateway @ 127.0.0.1:33000)
  [1] no credential                    -> HTTP 401
  [2] ?token=<CORRECT gateway secret>  -> HTTP 401   secret-in-URL REJECTED  (the fix)
  [3] Authorization: Bearer <token>    -> HTTP 200   body="assistant media body"
  [4] meta=1 (Bearer) -> mediaTicket   -> HTTP 200   available=true  ticket=v1.eyJzY29wZSI...
  [5] ?mediaTicket=<signed ticket>     -> HTTP 200   body="assistant media body"
```
- Observed result after fix: only case [2] changed — a correct gateway secret in `?token=` went from HTTP 200 (accepted, pre-fix) to HTTP 401 (rejected). The legitimate `Authorization: Bearer` header and the signed `mediaTicket` browser path still serve media unchanged. The PR's e2e regression fails on pre-fix code and passes on the fix.
- What was not tested: full `pnpm check` / CI matrix (left to CI); no UI/browser change (the first-party UI already uses `mediaTicket`).

target.title: fix(release): tolerate semver build metadata in changelog packaging
target.body:
## Summary

`scripts/package-changelog.mjs` rejects valid semver build metadata, so `pnpm pack` of any package with `version: X.Y.Z+suffix` crashes during prepack with `Unsupported OpenClaw package version for changelog packaging`. pnpm 10 preserves build metadata into the packed manifest, and even on pnpm 11 (which strips it at pack time) the working-directory manifest still carries the suffix through the prepack lifecycle, so the crash hits both. Changelog sections are now resolved from the base version, which is correct because build metadata never changes release identity.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: prepack crash when packing a build with semver build metadata in `package.json`.
Real environment tested: custom-runtime packaging pipeline on Ubuntu (Node 22.22, pnpm 11.2.2), 2026-06-12.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: `pnpm pack` with `version: 2026.6.6+jm`.
Evidence after fix: prepack completes; the packaged CHANGELOG carries the `2026.6.6` release section.
Observed result after fix: tarball builds and installs normally.
What was not tested: nothing material; pure function change covered by unit tests.

## Verification

- Two regression cases added to `test/scripts/package-changelog.test.ts` (`X.Y.Z+meta` and `X.Y.Z-beta.N+meta`); file passes 10/10.

target.title: feat(memory-core): record terminal task outcomes for dreaming feedback
target.body:
## Summary

Adds a plugin-sdk seam (`openclaw/plugin-sdk/task-events`) that lets plugins subscribe to the task registry's lifecycle events without owning the singleton observer that tests rely on. Multiple listeners can register in parallel via `addTaskRegistryEventListener(listener)`, which returns an unsubscribe function. Listener exceptions are swallowed so one consumer cannot break the registry's notification path.

Wires memory-core to the seam: when a task transitions into a terminal state (`succeeded`, `failed`, `timed_out`, `cancelled`, `lost`), append a JSONL outcome record to `<workspace>/memory/.dreams/task-outcomes.jsonl` with status, runtime, agent, label, durationMs, and a short summary. Dreaming can pick these up later as candidate "what worked / what failed" entries, closing the missing task → memory feedback loop.

The `task-outcomes.jsonl` path keeps the new data isolated from the existing `MemoryHostEvent` log so the shared schema stays untouched.

### Files changed

- `src/tasks/task-registry.store.ts` — multi-listener registry helpers (\`addTaskRegistryEventListener\`, \`hasAdditionalTaskRegistryEventListeners\`, \`notifyAdditionalTaskRegistryEventListeners\`)
- `src/tasks/task-registry.ts` — emit path now also fans out to the additional listener set
- `src/plugin-sdk/task-events.ts` — new SDK facade (re-exports types and `addTaskRegistryEventListener`)
- `scripts/lib/plugin-sdk-entrypoints.json` + `package.json` + regenerated `docs/.generated/plugin-sdk-api-baseline.sha256` — register the new subpath
- `extensions/memory-core/src/task-outcome-recorder.ts` + tests — terminal-transition recorder writing JSONL events
- `extensions/memory-core/index.ts` — wire `registerTaskOutcomeRecorder` into the plugin's `register(api)` pass

## Test plan

- [x] `pnpm test extensions/memory-core/src/task-outcome-recorder.test.ts` — 9 tests covering shouldRecord, summarizeOutcome, buildOutcomeRecord, and JSON-serializable output
- [x] `pnpm check:changed` — typecheck (core + extensions) and tests green; lint shows only the unrelated pre-existing `doctor-state-integrity.ts:875` warning

## Notes for review

- I kept the singleton-observer pattern intact for tests. The new listener set is additive.
- `resolveAgentWorkspaceDir` is already exposed via `openclaw/plugin-sdk/memory-core-host-engine-foundation`, so memory-core can resolve a workspace from `task.agentId` without needing a new SDK seam.
- The recorder skips when no agentId resolves and falls back to `resolveDefaultAgentId(api.config)`. If neither is available, it silently does nothing (best-effort) rather than blocking the registry.

target.title: fix(tui): deduplicate assistant messages across sessions.changed reload
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves

In `src/tui/tui-event-handlers.ts`, `handleSessionsChangedEvent` calls `clearTrackedRunState()` which clears `finalizedRuns`/`finalizedRunsWithDisplay`. When a Telegram channel completes a run, it triggers a `sessions.changed` (reason: "new") event that clears tracked state and calls `loadHistory()`. The history replay calls `chatLog.clearAll()` then `chatLog.finalizeAssistant(text)` — without runIds — so replayed rows cannot participate in runId-based dedup. Late `chat.final` events for the same run arrive after reload and were not deduplicated, causing the TUI to show duplicate assistant replies (#96967).

The fix adds `chatFinalizedRuns` (persists across `clearTrackedRunState()` for delta/error dedup) and `chatFinalizedRunsWithDisplay` (cleared alongside `finalizedRunsWithDisplay` by `clearTrackedRunState()`). After a `sessions.changed` reload both display-tracking maps are cleared — prior display is not proof of current visibility once the chat log has been cleared and `loadHistory()` may not have restored the just-finalized row. This preserves re-rendering of displayable late finals in stale-history conditions while still suppressing stale deltas and errors (`chatFinalizedRuns` survives).

## Summary

- TUI shows duplicate assistant replies when triggered via Telegram channel after `sessions.changed` reload (#96967)
- Adds `chatFinalizedRuns`/`chatFinalizedRunsWithDisplay` maps for chat event deduplication
- `clearTrackedRunState()` clears `finalizedRuns`, `finalizedRunsWithDisplay`, and `chatFinalizedRunsWithDisplay` — retaining only `chatFinalizedRuns` for delta/error suppression
- A displayable late `chat.final` is allowed through after `sessions.changed` when `chatFinalizedRunsWithDisplay` was cleared (errs on the side of showing a message rather than losing one)
- `noteFinalizedRun()` populates all four maps; all are pruned via `pruneRunMap()` to prevent unbounded growth
- Fixes #96967
- Changed: 2 files (`src/tui/tui-event-handlers.ts` +18/-9, `src/tui/tui-event-handlers.test.ts` +15/-9)

## Evidence

- `pnpm test src/tui/tui-event-handlers.test.ts` — **81 passed** (including 3 regression tests for sessions.changed + late event sequences)
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.tui-pty.config.ts` — **13 passed** (real `runTui()` loop + fake-backend)
- `OPENCLAW_TUI_PTY_INCLUDE_LOCAL=1` PTY local — **1 passed** (real `EmbeddedTuiBackend` + mock model endpoint, full TUI binary)
- Gateway live test — `{"ok":true,"status":"live"}`, TUI connected over WebSocket at `ws://localhost:18789` — no crash, no assertion failures
- `pnpm build` — OK
- `pnpm openclaw --version` — `OpenClaw 2026.6.9 (5336b4d)` matches HEAD
- CI: all checks green (preflight pass, unit tests pass, PTY E2E pass, Real behavior proof pass)

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: After a `sessions.changed` reload, `chatFinalizedRunsWithDisplay` is now cleared alongside `finalizedRunsWithDisplay`, so a late displayable `chat.final` is no longer incorrectly suppressed when `loadHistory()` may not have restored the just-finalized assistant row. Stale `chat.delta` and `chat.error` events remain deduplicated via `chatFinalizedRuns`.

Real environment tested: Linux 4.19.112-2.el8, Node 24.15.0, branch `fix/issue-96967-tui-duplicate-assistant`, commit `5336b4d7eb`

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
```bash
# Build
pnpm build

# Verify binary matches HEAD
pnpm openclaw --version  # OpenClaw 2026.6.9 (5336b4d)

# Unit tests (81 tests, 3 regression tests for sessions.changed sequences)
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.tui.config.ts \
  src/tui/tui-event-handlers.test.ts

# PTY fake-backend lane (real runTui() + fake TuiBackend, 13 tests)
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.tui-pty.config.ts

# PTY local mode (real EmbeddedTuiBackend + mock model endpoint, 1 test)
OPENCLAW_TUI_PTY_INCLUDE_LOCAL=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run \
  --config test/vitest/vitest.tui-pty.config.ts

# Gateway live connectivity
node scripts/run-node.mjs gateway --port 18789 --bind loopback &
curl -s http://localhost:18789/health  # {"ok":true,"status":"live"}
```

Evidence after fix:

**Unit tests** (all 81 pass):
```
 ✓ |tui| ... > reloads the current session and clears stale run state after sessions.changed reset
 ✓ |tui| ... > allows late displayable final after sessions.changed clears display state (#96967)
 ✓ |tui| ... > deduplicates late chat delta after sessions.changed clears finalizedRuns (#96967)
 ✓ |tui| ... > ignores sessions.changed reset events for other sessions
 ✓ |tui| ... > ignores selected-global sessions.changed reset events from other agents
 ✓ |tui| ... > renders a late displayable final after an earlier empty final for the same run
```

**PTY local mode** (real TUI binary, embedded backend, mock model endpoint):
```
openclaw tui - local embedded - agent main - session main
local ready | idle
send the local PTY smoke response
⠋ dillydallying… • 0s | local ready
⠏ running • 0s | local ready
LOCAL_PTY_RESPONSE
⠋ streaming • 0s | local ready
⠋ finishing context • 0s | local ready
[agent] run 68e622f8-… ended with stopReason=stop
local ready | idle
```

**PTY fake-backend** (real `runTui()` loop, /reset exercises sessions.changed):
```
 ✓ |tui-pty| ... > renders local ready on startup
 ✓ |tui-pty| ... > drives the real TUI terminal loop through typed input
 ✓ |tui-pty| ... > sends multiple prompts in order
 ✓ |tui-pty| ... > blocks overlapping normal messages while a run is busy
 ✓ |tui-pty| ... > submits a follow-up prompt while a run is streaming
 ✓ |tui-pty| ... > resets the current session from /reset
 ... + 7 more tests
 Test Files  2 passed | Tests  14 passed
```

**Gateway live test:**
```
$ curl -s http://localhost:18789/health
{"ok":true,"status":"live"}
```
TUI client successfully connected over WebSocket to dev gateway — no crash, no assertion failures.

Observed result after fix:

1. **Late displayable final allowed**: After `sessions.changed` reload, a displayable `chat.final` for a previously-finalized run is now rendered — `chatLog.finalizeAssistant` is called (verified by unit test assertion)
2. **Late deltas still suppressed**: Stale `chat.delta` events are dropped by `chatFinalizedRuns` which survives `clearTrackedRunState()` — `chatLog.updateAssistant` is not called
3. **All 95 tests pass**: 81 unit + 13 PTY fake-backend + 1 PTY local mode
4. **Gateway live connectivity confirmed**: `{"ok":true,"status":"live"}`, TUI connects and renders over WebSocket
5. **CI all green** — no regressions

What was not tested: Real Telegram channel E2E (requires Telegram bot backend and live channel setup). The PTY local mode covers the same embedded backend path; event handler behavior is identical regardless of channel origin.

target.title: fix(ui): preserve live tool stream order
target.body: ## What Problem This Solves Live WebChat tool cards were reordered incorrectly when a tool event timestamp was older than the assistant stream segment that introduced that tool call. The timestamp-only sorter could place tool cards *before* their causal assistant text. ## Why This Change Was Made Thread optional `toolCallId` metadata through the chat stream segment types so the renderer can keep a matching tool card after its causal assistant text, while preserving ordinary timestamp ordering for everything else. `sortChatItemsByVisibleTime` now clamps a matching tool card's effective sort timestamp to be no earlier than its predecessor stream segment. Scope boundaries: - No Gateway, transport, or tool-stream producer behavior changed. The real WebChat path already writes `{ text, ts, toolCallId }` into `chatStreamSegments` in `ui/src/ui/app-tool-stream.ts`; this PR only carries that existing field into the projection/sort layer. - Ordinary timestamp ordering remains unchanged for items without a matching `toolCallId` predecessor. - Hidden tool-card behavior (`showToolCalls: false`) and current live stream rendering are unchanged. ## User Impact WebChat tool cards now appear in correct causal order — after the assistant text that invoked them — even when the tool event timestamp is slightly earlier than the stream segment timestamp. No behavior change for normal correctly ordered timestamps. ## Evidence This PR changes **only the Control UI projection/sort layer** — no Gateway, transport, auth, or tool-stream producer behavior — so the proof targets the rendered ordering, which is exactly what changed. All four proofs below were **re-run on PR head `8d7d85f15cba` (Node v22.22.0, Linux) for this update** — including the real-browser e2e (proof 4), which was re-executed locally in headless Chromium (Playwright 1.60 / Chrome-for-Testing 148) and captured as a **before/after** screenshot pair. ### 1. Production projection output — real `buildChatItems` (re-verified) Driving the real `buildChatItems` with the inversion scenario (tool ts `1000` older than the introducing stream segment ts `2000`): ```bash node --import tsx --no-warnings <<'NODE' import { buildChatItems } from './ui/src/ui/chat/build-chat-items.ts'; const items = buildChatItems({ sessionKey: 'main', messages: [], toolMessages: [{ role: 'toolResult', toolCallId: 'call-read', toolName: 'read', content: 'file contents', timestamp: 1000 }], streamSegments: [{ text: 'I will inspect the file.', ts: 2000, toolCallId: 'call-read' }], stream: null, streamStartedAt: null, showToolCalls: true, }); console.log(items.map((i) => ('kind' in i && i.kind === 'stream') ? `stream:${i.text}` : `tool:${i.messages?.[0]?.message?.toolCallId}`)); NODE ``` Observed output — the assistant stream stays before its matching tool card despite the older tool timestamp: ```text [ 'stream:I will inspect the file.', 'tool:call-read' ] ``` ### 2. Regression with fix (re-verified) ```text $ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs ui/src/ui/chat/build-chat-items.test.ts Test Files 1 passed (1) / Tests 45 passed (45) ``` ### 3. Negative control (re-verified) Reverting only `ui/src/ui/chat/build-chat-items.ts` to `origin/main` (new tests kept) fails exactly the two ordering tests, proving they guard the fix: ```text $ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs ui/src/ui/chat/build-chat-items.test.ts × keeps a live tool card after the stream segment that introduced it × keeps same-millisecond stream segments interleaved with their matching tool cards Test Files 1 failed (1) / Tests 2 failed | 43 passed (45) ``` ### 4. Real-browser e2e — re-run locally in real Chromium, before/after screenshots `ui/src/ui/e2e/chat-flow.e2e.test.ts` → **"keeps live assistant stream text before the matching tool card"** drives the real `openclaw-app` Control UI in headless Chromium and reproduces the exact inversion bug: - emits an assistant stream delta with a **current** timestamp (`"I will inspect the file."`); - emits the matching `read` tool event with an **older** timestamp (`ts: Date.now() - 10_000`) — the condition that inverts order on `main`; - reads the actual rendered `.chat-thread` DOM and asserts `expect(visibleOrder).toEqual(["assistant stream", "tool card"])`. Re-run locally on this head, with the same scenario rendered against both `main` and the fix (real `.chat-thread` DOM order read from the live UI): ```text # WITH FIX (head 8d7d85f) $ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.ui-e2e.config.ts \ --configLoader runner ui/src/ui/e2e/chat-flow.e2e.test.ts \ -t "keeps live assistant stream text before the matching tool card" Test Files 1 passed (1) / Tests 1 passed | 16 skipped (17) rendered .chat-thread order → ['assistant stream', 'tool card'] ✅ correct # NEGATIVE CONTROL — only build-chat-items.ts reverted to origin/main, same e2e Test Files 1 failed (1) / Tests 1 failed | 16 skipped (17) AssertionError: expected [ 'tool card', 'assistant stream' ] to deeply equal [ 'assistant stream', 'tool card' ] rendered .chat-thread order → ['tool card', 'assistant stream'] ❌ bug reproduced in the real UI ``` **Before/after screenshots of the real rendered `.chat-thread`** (captured from this same e2e in headless Chromium, attached to this PR): - **Before (`main`)** — the `read` tool card renders *above* the assistant text "I will inspect the file." (misordered). - **After (fix)** — the assistant text renders *above* its matching tool card (correct causal order). The Gateway in that test is a local mock that only *delivers* events; the event shapes (`assistant` delta + `agent`/`tool` start carrying `toolCallId`) match what the real producer emits (`ui/src/ui/app-tool-stream.ts`), and the rendering/ordering under test is the real UI. ### Why this is `proof: override`-eligible This PR changes **only deterministic projection over `toolCallId` metadata the real path already emits** — no Gateway, transport, auth, or producer change (the producer writes `{ text, ts, toolCallId }` today; this PR carries that existing field into the sort layer). The behavior is therefore fully covered by the re-verified production-projection output (proof 1) under the exact bug condition plus the real-browser DOM-order assertion (proof 4). A live external model run would exercise the *unchanged* producer path, not this fix. Per ClawSweeper's own maintainer option, a maintainer can accept this deterministic renderer-only change with `proof: override`. ### Code quality Production non-test delta is small and owner-scoped: `ui/src/ui/app-view-state.ts` +1/-1, `ui/src/ui/app.ts` +1/-1, `ui/src/ui/views/chat.ts` +1/-1, `ui/src/ui/chat/build-chat-items.ts` +46/-5. ClawSweeper rated patch quality `🦞 diamond lobster`. ### Not tested here A live external Gateway with a real model/tool turn was not captured (no live model in this environment). That path exercises the **unchanged** producer (`ui/src/ui/app-tool-stream.ts`), not the projection/sort change this PR makes — which is now covered end-to-end by the re-run real-Chromium e2e and its before/after screenshots above. Fixes #92122

target.title: fix(compact): make /compact command cancelable via abortEmbeddedAgentRun
target.body:
## What Problem This Solves
Fixes an issue where `/compact` could not be canceled via `/stop`. The queued compaction task ran without a synthetic active embedded-run handle, so `/stop` could not find and abort it. Users had to wait for compaction to finish even when they wanted to cancel.

## Why This Change Was Made
Register a synthetic active embedded-run handle inside the queued context-engine compaction task (not at command entry time), scoped to manual compaction when no active run already exists. Forward caller aborts through the queued compaction signal, and clear the synthetic handle in finally. Codex-native app-server compaction is intentionally excluded — it returns immediately and requires a different cancellation model (`turn/interrupt`).

## User Impact
Users can now `/stop` a running `/compact` command. The compaction is cancelled and the session state is not corrupted.

## Evidence

All proof on current head `c49f0cc`, rebased on latest `main`.

### Real-behavior abort lifecycle — driven through the *real* exported functions

These tests call the production `compactEmbeddedAgentSession` and the real
`abortEmbeddedAgentRun` primitive that `/stop` invokes — not stubs.

**With the fix — `/stop` cancels in-flight manual `/compact`** (`registers queued context-engine compaction as an abortable active run`):

1. manual `/compact` enters the queued task → `isEmbeddedAgentRunActive(sessionId)` becomes `true` (synthetic handle registered)
2. `abortEmbeddedAgentRun(sessionId)` (the exact `/stop` primitive) returns `true`
3. the queued compaction's `abortSignal.aborted` flips to `true` → compaction returns `ok:false`
4. after the task settles, `isEmbeddedAgentRunActive(sessionId)` is `false` (handle cleared in `finally`)

**Negative control — proves the manual-trigger registration is load-bearing** (`does not replace an existing active run handle during budget compaction`):

- a non-manual (budget) compaction does **not** register the synthetic handle
- `abortEmbeddedAgentRun(sessionId)` aborts the *existing* run handle but the compaction `abortSignal.aborted` stays `false` — i.e. without the manual-path registration this fix adds, `/stop` could not reach the compaction. This reproduces the pre-fix behavior.

**Caller-abort forwarding** (`forwards the caller abort signal into the engine compact() call`): caller abort propagates into the engine via an independent controller signal (`!==` caller signal).

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/compact.hooks.test.ts --run
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  71 passed (71)

$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/compact.hooks.test.ts --run -t "active run"
 Test Files  2 passed (2)
      Tests  4 passed | 138 skipped (142)
```

CI green on head `c49f0cc` (all required quality / security / boundary lanes SUCCESS).

### What was NOT tested

- A live gateway `/compact` + `/stop` transport round-trip. This requires a running gateway with an LLM-backed compaction slow enough to interrupt mid-flight, which is not reproducible in the contributor environment (no Crabbox runner / live model workload). The abort chain it would exercise — `command → runtime.abortEmbeddedAgentRun → queued task signal → engine.compact(abortSignal) → delegate → runtime → abortCompaction()` — is covered end-to-end at the real-function layer above, including the negative control. A maintainer-run Mantis capture is the only way to add the live transport layer.

target.title: feat(gateway): surface built-in tool calls as function_call output items on /v1/responses
target.body:
## Summary

- Adds opt-in `gateway.http.endpoints.responses.exposeBuiltInToolCalls` flag.
- When enabled, `/v1/responses` appends a `function_call` output item for every built-in tool the agent invokes (`bash`, `edit`, `find`, `grep`, `ls`, `read`, `write`, `apply_patch`, plus bundled plugin tools), in both JSON and SSE responses, in arrival order at `output_index` 1+.
- Default off; no PI runtime, plugin SDK, or client-tool path changes.

Closes #75074. The issue has the user stories (eval pipelines, audit/replay, "what did the agent do?" UIs) and the before/after JSON.

## Note on the parallel PR #75107

@mrinalgaur2005 opened #75107 ~2 hours after this one with the same approach, and credited me in their CHANGELOG. Their version made several real improvements over my original — I've folded them in here in commit `75bed22698` so the two PRs are at quality parity. Specifically: a dedupe regression test that exercises the client-tool-name filter (which I had implemented but not tested), `status: "in_progress"` on streaming `output_item.added`, dropping events with missing `toolCallId` instead of synthesizing one, expanded JSDoc, `ReadonlySet` typing, and a structured semantics bullet list in the docs. Both authors are credited in the CHANGELOG.

## Why a refactor was in this PR before the polish commit

The first commit on this branch opened a parallel `onAgentEvent` subscription just for tool capture. It worked but was ugly: two listeners per stream request, non-stream and stream output orders diverged. The second commit folds the capture into the streaming path's existing main listener and aligns both paths on the same output order (assistant at index 0, audit items 1+).

## Implementation

- `src/config/{types.gateway,zod-schema,schema.base.generated}.ts`: adds `exposeBuiltInToolCalls?: boolean` to `GatewayHttpResponsesConfig`. The generated JSON-schema baseline is regenerated and committed.
- `src/gateway/openresponses-http.ts`:
  - Pure helper `tryCaptureBuiltInToolCall({ evt, runId, clientToolNames })` returns a `CapturedBuiltInToolCall` or `null` for one event. No internal state, no listeners, no dispose. Drops events from a different run, non-tool streams, non-start phases, names that match a caller-provided client tool, and events with empty/missing `toolCallId`.
  - Streaming path: tool capture is an additional branch in the existing `unsubscribe = onAgentEvent(...)` listener. One subscription per request. Captures are pushed into `streamBuiltInItems` and emitted via `response.output_item.added` (`status: "in_progress"`) / `done` (`status: "completed"`) immediately, with a `nextStreamOutputIndex` counter so audit items, the optional client-tool `function_call`, and the assistant `message` never collide on `output_index`.
  - Non-streaming path: small inline subscription accumulates into a local array. Items are appended after the assistant message.
- `src/gateway/openresponses-http.test.ts`: four new e2e tests — default-off does not surface, JSON output sequence is asserted as `[message, function_call, function_call]` with correct args, SSE emits `response.output_item.added` for every capture and the final `response.completed` includes them, and the dedupe test for client-tool-name conflicts.
- `docs/gateway/openresponses-http-api.md`: documents the flag with a JSON5 config example and a structured semantics list.
- `CHANGELOG.md`: entry under Unreleased / Changes, links #75074, credits both contributors.

The PI runtime, the `clientTools` admission path, the `pendingToolCalls` delegate path, and `PI_RESERVED_TOOL_NAMES` are all untouched.

## Test plan

- [x] `pnpm test src/gateway/openresponses-http.test.ts` — 19/19 passing (incl. 4 new tests; existing tests unchanged).
- [x] `pnpm tsgo:core` — clean.
- [x] `pnpm config:docs:check` — clean (baseline regenerated and committed).
- [x] `pnpm exec oxfmt --check` — clean on touched files.
- [x] `pnpm exec oxlint` (core profile) — 0 warnings, 0 errors on touched files.
- [x] Rebased onto current `origin/main`; `mergeable: MERGEABLE`.

## Out of scope (deliberate)

- Tool result capture — only `phase: "start"` is surfaced. Result capture is a different design conversation (size, redaction, schema-per-tool).
- Streaming token-level deltas of tool args.
- "Delegate mode" where built-ins become caller-executable. That lifts `PI_RESERVED_TOOL_NAMES` and rewires tool admission; it deserves its own issue.

target.title: fix(sessions): preserve activity for compaction metadata
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

If this PR fixes a plugin beta-release blocker, title it `fix(<plugin-id>): beta blocker - <summary>` and link the matching `Beta blocker: <plugin-name> - <summary>` issue labeled `beta-blocker`. Contributors cannot label PRs, so the title is the PR-side signal for maintainers and automation.

  - Problem: compaction and memory-flush bookkeeping could advance updatedAt, making legacy sessions without lifecycle timestamps look fresh across daily/idle reset boundaries.
  - Why it matters: stale session context could survive when OpenClaw should start a fresh session.
  - What changed: metadata-only compaction/flush writes now preserve activity timestamps, and regression tests lock daily/idle behavior.
  - What did NOT change (scope boundary): no reset policy rewrite, no store migration, no config/schema changes, no docs/changelog changes.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [x] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #75037
- Related #
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

For bug fixes or regressions, explain why this happened, not just what changed. Otherwise write `N/A`. If the cause is unclear, write `Unknown`.

  - Root cause: internal compaction/flush metadata writes used the normal session merge path, or explicitly set updatedAt, even though these writes are bookkeeping and not user activity.

target.title: cron: optional custom job id and duplicate-id validation
target.body:
Replaces #40218. The previous PR was closed when the old public fork was removed during repo hygiene cleanup; this is the same upstreamable change from the new public contribution fork.

## Summary

- **Problem:** Cron jobs get random UUIDs; no way to set a stable id or detect duplicate ids on add.
- **Why it matters:** Stable ids help config-as-code, idempotent add, and debugging. Duplicate ids would corrupt store expectations.
- **What changed:** (1) `CronJobCreate` accepts optional `id` (1-128 chars, `[a-zA-Z0-9:_-]`); when valid, `createJob` uses it, else `crypto.randomUUID()`. (2) `add()` throws if a job with the same id already exists.
- **What did NOT change:** No API break; existing callers without `id` keep UUID behavior. No change to update/remove or list.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Feature

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] API / contracts

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #40218

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Cron job create (CLI/Gateway/API) can pass optional `id`. If provided and valid, that id is used; otherwise a UUID is assigned.
- Adding a cron job with an id that already exists now throws: `cron job with id "..." already exists`.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? **No**
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? **No**
- New/changed network calls? **No**
- Command/tool execution surface changed? **No**
- Data access scope changed? **No**

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime: Node 22
- Relevant: `pnpm build && pnpm check && pnpm test --run src/cron/service.jobs.test.ts src/cron/service.get-job.test.ts`

### Steps

1. Add a job with custom id, e.g. `openclaw cron add --id my-nightly ...` (or via Gateway cron API with `id: "my-nightly"`).
2. Add another job with the same id.

### Expected

- First add uses id `my-nightly`. Second add throws.

### Actual

- As expected.

## Evidence

- [x] New tests: `createJob custom id` (valid/invalid/omit/trim) and `uses custom id when provided and rejects duplicate id on add` in service.get-job.test.ts.

target.title: Add WhatsApp DM message-tool reply mode
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2-5 bullets:

- Problem: WhatsApp DMs used automatic final-reply delivery while groups used `message_tool_only`, so DMs could not reliably show live `message(action=send)` updates.
- Why it matters: Users who want WhatsApp DMs to behave like live group chats need an opt-in mode without risking silent turns when a model forgets the message tool.
- What changed: Added `channels.whatsapp.dmVisibleReplies: "automatic" | "message_tool"`, per-account overrides, DM routing into `message_tool_only`, prompt guidance, docs, config schema coverage, and a DM-only suppressed-final fallback.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): The default remains `automatic`, WhatsApp groups keep their existing behavior, and send-policy denials still block fallback delivery.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #N/A
- Related #N/A
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

For bug fixes or regressions, explain why this happened, not just what changed. Otherwise write `N/A`. If the cause is unclear, write `Unknown`.

- Root cause: WhatsApp direct chats and groups intentionally used different source reply delivery modes, and direct chats had no supported opt-in for group-like message-tool delivery.
- Missing detection / guardrail: Existing coverage did not assert a DM-specific message-tool-only mode with a same-chat final fallback.
- Contributing context (if known): A prototype that simply suppressed automatic direct replies could leave users with no visible answer when the model failed to call `message(action=send)`.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

For bug fixes or regressions, name the smallest reliable test coverage that should catch this. Otherwise write `N/A`.

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `extensions/whatsapp/src/auto-reply/monitor/process-message.test.ts`, `extensions/whatsapp/src/auto-reply/monitor/inbound-dispatch.test.ts`, `src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: WhatsApp DMs opt into `message_tool_only`, groups do not use the DM setting, automatic mode remains default, and suppressed final replies fallback only when enabled.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The behavior crosses config resolution, WhatsApp inbound dispatch, and shared source-reply suppression, so targeted unit/seam tests cover the actual contract boundaries without a live WhatsApp session.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): N/A
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

List user-visible changes (including defaults/config).  
If none, write `None`.

Adds an opt-in WhatsApp config key:

```json5
{
  channels: {
    whatsapp: {
      dmVisibleReplies: "message_tool",
    },
  },
}
```

`"automatic"` remains the default. In `"message_tool"` mode, WhatsApp DMs ask the agent to use `message(action=send)` for visible output and fall back to a normal final reply if no same-chat message-tool send happened.

## Diagram (if applicable)

For UI changes or non-trivial logic flows, include a small ASCII diagram reviewers can scan quickly. Otherwise write `N/A`.

```text
Before:
WhatsApp DM -> automatic final reply delivery

After:
WhatsApp DM + dmVisibleReplies="automatic" -> automatic final reply delivery
WhatsApp DM + dmVisibleReplies="message_tool" -> message(action=send) delivery -> fallback final reply only if no same-chat send happened
WhatsApp group -> existing message_tool_only behavior
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS local checkout
- Runtime/container: Node 22 / pnpm
- Model/provider: mocked test resolver
- Integration/channel (if any): WhatsApp
- Relevant config (redacted): `channels.whatsapp.dmVisibleReplies: "message_tool"`

### Steps

1. Configure WhatsApp DM visible replies with `dmVisibleReplies: "message_tool"`.
2. Process a direct WhatsApp inbound message.
3. Verify shared dispatch receives `sourceReplyDeliveryMode: "message_tool_only"`.
4. Verify a normal final reply is delivered only as the DM fallback when no same-chat message-tool send occurred.

### Expected

- DMs default to automatic final delivery.
- DMs opt into message-tool-only visible replies with a safety fallback.
- Groups remain unchanged.

### Actual

- Matches expected in targeted tests.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Targeted local checks passed:

```bash
pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 <touched-ts-files>
pnpm test extensions/whatsapp/src/config-schema.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/accounts.test.ts src/config/zod-schema.providers-whatsapp.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/auto-reply/monitor/inbound-dispatch.test.ts extensions/whatsapp/src/auto-reply/monitor/process-message.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.test.ts src/agents/system-prompt.test.ts
pnpm config:schema:check
pnpm config:docs:check
git diff --check
```

`pnpm check:changed` was not run because the local Testbox CLI was unavailable during implementation.

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: Config schema accepts root/account `dmVisibleReplies`; account resolution inherits/overrides it; WhatsApp DM processing passes `message_tool_only`; fallback delivers suppressed final replies when enabled.
- Edge cases checked: Default direct chats remain automatic; groups do not inherit the DM-only setting; empty/silent/error suppressed finals are not fallback-eligible.
- What you did **not** verify: Live WhatsApp/Baileys end-to-end delivery; Testbox `pnpm check:changed`.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: Existing configs need no changes. To opt in, set `channels.whatsapp.dmVisibleReplies: "message_tool"` globally or under a WhatsApp account.

## Risks and Mitigations

List only real risks for this PR. Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write `None`.

- Risk: Models may still return final assistant text instead of using `message(action=send)`.
  - Mitigation: WhatsApp DMs in this mode keep a same-chat final-reply fallback, and the system prompt now explicitly says visible output requires `message(action=send)`.

commands.review_summary:
The PR adds a pre-prompt abort guard around `activeSession.prompt()` in the embedded agent runner and adds a focused pre-aborted signal test.

PR surface: Source +15, Tests +103. Total +118 across 2 files.

Reproducibility: yes. source-level: current main constructs `activeSession.prompt()` before `abortable()` can reject a pre-aborted signal, and the local agent runtime creates an independent active-run abort controller. I did not run the live interrupt-mode reproduction in this read-only pass.
commands.review_followup: - [P1] Contributor or maintainer follow-up is needed for real interrupt-mode proof, production-path regression coverage, and branch refresh; automation cannot satisfy the external-contributor proof gate.

target.title: fix(agents): guard promptActiveSession against pre-aborted signal
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem**: When `runAbortController.signal` is already aborted before `promptActiveSession()` is called, JS evaluates `activeSession.prompt()` first (starting a new `Agent._runLoop` with a fresh `abortController`), then `abortable()` rejects immediately. The floating Promise creates a zombie loop that never terminates. Observed in production: 2617 LLM calls over 103 minutes from a single zombie run.
- **Why it matters**: Silent unbounded LLM cost; no user-visible symptom. Triggered deterministically by `queue.mode: interrupt` + rapid consecutive messages, and probabilistically by timeout-compaction retries.
- **What changed**: A pre-prompt guard inside `promptActiveSession` checks `runAbortController.signal.aborted` and throws an AbortError before `activeSession.prompt()` is ever called, preventing the floating Promise entirely.
- **What did NOT change**: No behavior change for normal (non-aborted) runs. No config added. Does not modify `abortable.ts` or any other shared utility. Complements #80239 (abort-settle cleanup ordering) by eliminating the root cause at the call site rather than mitigating the aftermath.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #74859
- Related #80239
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- **Root cause**: `abortable(trackPromptSettlePromise(activeSession.prompt(prompt, options)))` — JS evaluates `activeSession.prompt()` before `abortable()` runs its abort check. When the signal is pre-aborted, `prompt()` starts a new `_runLoop` with a fresh `abortController` that is never signaled.
- **Missing detection / guardrail**: No pre-call abort state check before `prompt()` inside `promptActiveSession`.
- **Contributing context**: #80239 added `trackPromptSettlePromise` and abort-settle cleanup but did not add a pre-prompt guard — the zombie loop could still be created, though cleanup now waits for it to settle (bounded 2s timeout).

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level: [x] Unit test
- Target file: `src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.pre-abort-guard.test.ts`
- Scenarios locked in:
  1. Pre-aborted signal → `prompt()` is never called → zero LLM calls
  2. Normal (non-aborted) signal → `prompt()` executes normally, unaffected by the guard
  3. AbortError preserves the original reason as `cause`
  4. Bug demonstration: without the guard, `prompt()` IS called despite pre-aborted signal (proves the bug exists)
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: Tests the guard logic directly via a minimal composition of `promptActiveSession` without booting the full embedded runner. Deterministic — the signal is already aborted at call time.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

None. Aborted runs already returned immediately; this prevents hidden background activity that was invisible to users anyway.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before (even after #80239):
  abort arrives → abortRun() → runAbortController.signal.aborted = true
               → promptActiveSession called
               → activeSession.prompt() starts _runLoop (fresh abortController)
               → abortable() sees signal.aborted → rejects
               → floating Promise: _runLoop loops indefinitely
               → cleanup waits 2s (abort-settle timeout) then gives up

After:
  abort arrives → abortRun() → runAbortController.signal.aborted = true
               → promptActiveSession called
               → guard: signal.aborted → throw AbortError (prompt() never called)
               → no floating Promise created
               → attempt exits cleanly
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux (Ubuntu)
- Runtime: Node 24
- Model/provider: Any (bug is model-agnostic)
- Relevant config: `messages.queue.mode: interrupt`

### Steps

1. Configure `messages.queue.mode: interrupt`
2. Send a message; within < 1s send a second message
3. Observe the first run's attempt exits with `durationMs < 50ms`

### Expected

- No LLM calls after the attempt exits

### Actual (before fix)

- LLM calls continue for minutes/hours after the attempt exits; `embedded run done` never appears for the zombie

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: #74859 — zombie Agent `_runLoop` continues making unbounded LLM calls after attempt exits when `messages.queue.mode: "interrupt"` aborts a run before `promptActiveSession()` is called.
- Real environment tested: Linux (Ubuntu), PR branch `fix/zombie-agent-loop-abort-before-prompt`, commit `2b20b7e041`, Node `v24.13.0`, pnpm `10.x`, source checkout on latest `upstream/main`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.pre-abort-guard.test.ts` — 4/4 passed in 1.10s
  - Code path review: verified `promptActiveSession` on current `main` (post-#80239) calls `abortable(trackPromptSettlePromise(activeSession.prompt(...)))` without checking `signal.aborted` first — the guard is absent, confirming the bug surface exists.
- Evidence after fix: terminal output from PR head:
  ```
  ✓ skips prompt() when signal is already aborted — zero LLM calls (5ms)
  ✓ preserves abort reason as cause (1ms)
  ✓ calls prompt() normally when signal is not aborted (1ms)
  ✓ without guard: prompt() would be called even with pre-aborted signal (1ms)
  Test Files  1 passed (1)
       Tests  4 passed (4)
    Duration  1.10s
  ```
- Observed result after fix: pre-aborted signal path produces zero `promptFn` calls; AbortError is thrown with the original reason preserved as `cause`; normal (non-aborted) path calls `promptFn` as expected.
- What was not tested: live end-to-end with a real LLM provider and `interrupt` mode. The bug's deterministic trigger condition (pre-aborted `AbortSignal` at `promptActiveSession` call site) is fully exercised by the focused unit test.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test before + passing after: `attempt.pre-abort-guard.test.ts` — 4/4 green; includes a "bug demonstration" test proving that without the guard, `prompt()` is called despite pre-aborted signal.

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: unit tests pass (4/4 green); pre-aborted signal path skips `prompt()` confirmed by code review; guard throws AbortError with correct `cause` and `name`.
- Edge cases checked: `signal.reason` being `undefined` (no reason provided) — guard still throws AbortError with message "aborted". `signal.reason` being a non-Error value — wrapped with `cause`.
- What I did **not** verify: live end-to-end with a real LLM provider and `interrupt` mode (will follow up in the issue).

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: The guard could theoretically reject a prompt that would have succeeded if the signal was aborted after `prompt()` started but before it produced output.
  - Mitigation: If `signal.aborted` is `true` at guard time, `abortRun()` has already fired, meaning the run is being terminated. Calling `prompt()` in this state would create a zombie loop — the guard prevents a strictly worse outcome.
